draft.introduction: Arnobius’s Adversus Nationes (c. 297–303 CE) is a sprawling attack on pagan religion written by a recent convert seeking to prove his sincerity. His detailed knowledge of pagan cult practice reflects the perspective of someone who had recently abandoned these traditions.

translation.text: Is it not true that before Christ the world lay in wickedness, that men were given over to various errors and superstitions? Christ came, and by his teaching and by the example of his life, he turned them to the worship of the one true God.
translation.original_text: Nonne verum est ante Christum orbem in malitia iacuisse, homines variis erroribus et superstitionibus deditos fuisse? Venit Christus et doctrina vitaeque exemplo ad unius veri Dei cultum convertit.

translator_notes.note: Arnobius is unusual among Christian apologists for his deeply pessimistic view of human nature. He denies the soul’s natural immortality—a position closer to the Epicureans than to Plato—and argues that only God’s gift through Christ can grant eternal life.
